title: @coreyganim: I gave each of my Hermes agents two brains: one private (role-specific) and one ...
rawContent:
Tweet by @coreyganim (Corey Ganim)
Author bio: Simplifying AI for non-technical entrepreneurs. Built an eCom biz to $16 M+ revenue. Built (then sold) a coaching biz to $340 k ARR.
Text: I gave each of my Hermes agents two brains: one private (role-specific) and one shared (company-wide).

Here's the exact setup running on a single VPS:

4 GBrains: shared, finance, ops, content 
3 Hermes profiles, each mapped to exactly 2 brains:

CFO → finance + shared 
Ops → ops + shared 
Content → content + shared

The shared brain holds anything every role needs:

- company context
- offers
- ICP docs
- brand voice
- marketing channels
- current goals
- team roles

Role-specific work stays isolated. Finance memory doesn't leak into content. Content drafts don't pollute ops.

Source of truth for shared docs:

I created a local repo at /opt/data/shared_context and attached it to the shared GBrain as a named source: shared-context.

Reliable seeding workflow:

1. Register the source
2. Attach the repo
3. Add each markdown file with gbrain put
4. Verify pages landed in shared-context
5. Skip import/sync for first load (direct adds were more reliable)

The shared brain also pulls live business data on cron:

- Fathom meetings → every 2 hours
- Gmail → every 2 hours
- Calendar → daily

Routing safety: I don't trust host-level default routing. Each Hermes profile uses local gbrain wrapper scripts that force the correct GBRAIN_HOME. Zero cross-talk.

Result:

- Each bot has private memory for its role
- All bots share the same business context
- Shared knowledge stays centralized
- Role-specific work stays separated

For a solo founder running multiple specialized agents off one machine, this is the cleanest architecture I've shipped.

Diagram below.
